courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Why Culture Matters
courses.description:
This course is an introduction to culture, with an aim to broaden students' understanding of humanities in general as well as the relevance of culture to contemporary society. The course explores the relationships among cultural appreciation, cultural representation, cultural continuity and cultural criticism; it also examines how related knowledge and practices in the humanities are valuable of enriching, on the one hand, students' own value system and lifestyle, and, on the other hand, the overall betterment of society, particularly in the digital age of globalization. The course is not theory-based, but its purpose is to expose the students to the diverse strains of cultural thought and practice that are relevant to contemporary society, particularly that of Hong Kong, China and Asia. Relevant contemporary issues will be drawn extensively to explain the values of the humanities and the need for knowledge of cultural studies in the societies of Hong Kong, China and Asia.

The course covers four sections to explain the breadth of knowledge that can be applied to understand how culture works. The four sections include (1) basic introduction of cultural studies as an interdisciplinary field, (2) media representation in popular culture, (3) memory, history and modernity and (4) community, urban culture and critical pedagogy. The four sections show the trajectory of applying a cultural studies point of view to understand why culture matters in today’s fast changing world. The Chinese and English sections of the course will select from a total of 22 sub-themes to discuss the role of culture to art, technology, education and humanities.
courses.learning_outcomes:
• Students will understand that culture is important in the building of a better society.

• They will also gain the ability in critically analyzing the diversity of values and interests in society.

• Students will be able to draw on a number of disciplinary origins within areas of the arts, humanities, and social sciences to appreciate the richness and the usefulness of interdisciplinary approach.

• Students will gain a critical respect for local histories, traditions, and appreciate the value of culture in humanities.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Section 1. Introduction: Cultural Studies and the Sociology of the Arts**

1. What is Cultural Studies?

2. Cultural Studies in Hong Kong, China and Asia

3. Language, Communication and Culture

4. Arts in Society

* *Section 2. Understanding Global Media and Popular Culture**

5. Popular Culture, Carnival and the Practice of Everyday Life

6. From Popular Culture to Art: The Case of Graffiti

7. Mass Media and Modes of Information

8. Cinema, Movie Stars and Literary Adaptation

9. Animation, Comics and Fan Culture

10. Gender Stereotypes in Media Representation: The Case of Disney Animation

11. The Transmedia Turn in Popular Video Games

* *Section 3. Memory, History and Modernity**

12. Love, Intimacy and Modernity

13. Enlightenment, Sentiment/Qing (情) and Public Sphere in Modern China

14. Nostalgia and Urban Culture: Hong Kong and Shanghai

15. East Asian Urban Culture in Cinema, TV Drama and Literature

16. The Exploitation of Love: Feminist Critique of Patriarchy and Capitalism

17. Techno Culture and Social Change

* *Section 4. Community, Urban Culture and Critical Pedagogy**

18. Culture on Display: Cultural Conservation and World Heritage

19. Collective Memory and Local Community

20. Diaspora and Homeland

21. Arts Education and Community Building

22. Critical Pedagogy and Dialogic Pedagogy

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Introduction to Public Humanities

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 408
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 408
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 402
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 402

courses.description: This course introduces students to the basic concepts and practices of public humanities, an emerging field that examines the influence of the humanities in the public sphere. It guides students through the ways in which humanistic thinking and inquiry can impact community engagement, collective cultural history and memory, policy-making, and the cultivation of empathy. Organized into three sections—Visions, Paths, and Experiments—each part focusing on different dimensions of public humanities, the course begins by exploring the conceptual foundations of public humanism, emphasizing the importance of curiosity, generosity, and collaboration. As the course progresses, students engage with real-world case studies that demonstrate how humanities initiatives can reshape culture and society. Students will also experiment with designing a small-scale collaborative project, which allows them to transform their public concerns and humanistic inquiries into concrete outcomes. These projects serve as a starting point for their further development within and beyond the programs of public humanities. The course is designed for students interested in leveraging the humanities to enrich public culture.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Upon successful completion of this course, students should be able to:*

1. *Analyze and Develop Public Humanities Projects:*

* Evaluate and curate small-scale public humanities projects, understanding their impact on community engagement and public scholarship.*

2. *Communicate Scholarly Research Effectively:*

* Utilize digital platforms to articulate complex ideas in a clear, accessible manner, effectively reaching and engaging diverse audiences.*

3. *Collaborate Effectively in Diverse Teams:*

* Work collaboratively to integrate diverse perspectives, assess team dynamics critically, and manage project challenges efficiently.*
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Part A: Visions**

* *Week 1: Rethinking the** **Humanities for a Better Future**

* Students will be introduced to the development of the humanities, focusing on the challenges in universities and exploring new directions.
* Core readings:

* Wang Hui, “The Humanities in China: History and Challenges.” *History of Humanities* 5, no. 2 (2020): 309-331.
* Judith Butler, “The Public Futures of the Humanities,” *Daedalus* 151, no. 3 (2022): 40-53.

* *Week 2: Being a Humanist: Learning to Be Curious**

* Students will learn about the tradition of humanism, especially the role of curiosity in everyday life as a counter to instrumental logic.
* Core readings:

* Sōetsu Yanagi, “The Beauty of Miscellaneous Things,” in *The Beauty of Everyday Things* (London: Penguin, 2018), 27-57.
* Perry Zurn and Dani Bassett, “A New Vantage Point,” in *Curious Minds: The Power of Connection* (Cambridge: MIT Press, 2023), 1-18.

* *Week 3: Being a Public Humanist: Learning to Be Collaborative**

* Students will explore the importance of generosity and collaboration as a counter to solipsism and competition.
* Core readings:

* Kathleen Fitzpatrick, “Reading Together,” in *Generous Thinking*(Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 2021), 82-131.
* Patricia Leavy, “Methods for Working with Nonacademic Stakeholders, Teams, and Communities,” in *Popularizing Scholarly Research* (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2021), 1-11.

* *Part B: Paths**

* *Week 4: Looking Around Us**

* Students will examine how public concerns are integral in shaping the environment and community.
* Core readings:

* Phillip Meuser, “How Architecture Supports Lives,” in *Public Humanities in Architecture* (Berlin: DOM Publisher, 2023) ***OR***
* Julie Sze et al., “Stories from the Field: Public Engagement through the Environmental Humanities and Allied Disciplines.” *Resilience: A Journal of the Environmental Humanities* 5, no. 2 (2018): 49-73.

* Tutorial Case Study:

* Suzannie Leung, “Socially Engaged Arts: Luke Ching’s Practices for Hong Kong,” *The International Journal of Social, Political and Community Agendas in the Arts* 14, no. 4 (2019): 25-35.

* *Week 5: Learning From the Past**

* Students will explore how history and memory are shaped and maintained through scholarship with a public dimension.
* Core readings:

* David Graeber, “The Myth of Barter,” in *Debt: The First 5000 Years* (London: Penguin, 2012), 21-41. ***OR***
* Simone Bohn and Pınar Melis Yelsalı Parmaksız “Researching Mothers in Public and Political Life,” in *Mothers in Public and Political Life* (Ontario: Demeter Press 2017)

* Tutorial Case Study:

* Constance Crompton, “SemantiQueer: Making Linked Data Worked for Public History.” In Schwan, Anne, and Tara Thomson, eds. *The Palgrave Handbook of Digital and Public Humanities* (Palgrave Macmillan, 2022), 89-104.

* *Week 6: Enriching Public Policy**

* Students will be introduced how humanists enrich the direction and quality of policymaking, especially through introducing alternative rhetoric, ideas, and styles.

* Core readings:

* Sarah Dillon and Claire Craig, “Introduction,” in *Storylistening: Narrative Evidence and Public Reasoning* (London: Routledge, 2021). ***OR***
* Judy Segal. “Public Discourse and Public Policy: Some Ways that Metaphor Constrains Health (Care).” *Journal of Medical Humanities* 18, no. 4 (1997): 217-231.

* Tutorial Case Study:

* Doris Sommer, “From the Top: Government-Sponsored Creativity,” in *The Work of Art in the World: Civic Agency and Public Humanities* (Durham: Duke University Press, 2013). ***OR***
* Mara Cristina Caballero, “Academic Turns City (Bogotá, Columbia) into a Social Experiment,” *The Harvard Gazette* (2004, March 11)

* *Week 7: Cultivating Empathy**

* Students will be introduced to how the humanities can offer empathy to the public.
* Core readings:

* Susan Lanzoni, “Introduction,” in *Empathy: A History* (New Haven: Yale University Press, 2018). ***OR***
* Xu Bin, “Introduction,” in *The Politics of Compassion: The Sichuan Earthquake and Civic Engagement in China* (Stanford: Stanford University Press, 2017).

* Tutorial Case Study

* Junxi Qian and Eric Florence, “Migrant Worker Museums in China: Public Cultures of Migrant Labour in State and Grassroots Initiatives,” in *New Theoretical Dialogues on Migration in China* (London: Routledge, 2023), 22-40.

* *Week 8: Reading Week and Midterm Paper Submission**

The midterm paper enables students to consolidate their learning from previous lectures, while also assisting them in completing their group projects and final papers. Students are required to engage with the course materials by addressing the following sections, with each section being approximately 300 words, for a total of no more than 1000 words:

* What aspects of your everyday life spark your curiosity? (Week 2)
* Describe the roles and responsibilities you typically assume in a collaborative project, such as:

* Studying community and/or environment
* Conducting historical investigations
* Analyzing public policy
* Communicating with diverse groups in the public sphere. (Weeks 3 to 7)

* The use of ChatGPT is permitted. You must, however, explain how ChatGPT was utilized in the preparation of your midterm paper, including details of the prompts given to the AI and how its responses facilitated your work.

* *Part C: Experiments: Curating** **a Small Humanistic Investigation for the Public**

* *Week 9****: Finding****, Reading****, and Curating** **Scholarly Works Online**

* Under the guidance of the instructor, students will collaborate to read and curate a humanistic investigation using recent academic journal articles on public issues such as:

* Environment and Sustainability
* Community and Inclusiveness
* Public Health and Aging
* Urban Development
* Labor Condition
* Mobility (e.g. Migration and Tourism)
* Inequality of Technology (e.g. Digital divide and AI debates)
* Other topics (after discussing with the instructor)

* Students will learn to interpret the latest research articles and curate them in an engaging direction for public consumption. The selected articles should be interdisciplinary, encompassing both humanities and non-humanities scholarly work, aimed at drawing public attention and enhancing public literacy and intelligence.
* The curated content will be refined in subsequent weeks and prepared for online publication on the social media account of the public humanities program.
* Core readings:

* Jamie Smith, “Undergraduate Research and the Public Humanities: Workable Futures.” *Pedagogy* 22, no. 1 (2022): 61-78. ***OR***
* David Rowe, “The University as a ‘Giant Newsroom’: The Uses of Academic Knowledge Revisited,” *Culture Unbound* 9, no. 3 (2017): 228-239.

* Tutorial exercise:

* Students will be grouped and tasked with developing a tentative area or title for their project.

* *Week 10: Impactful Stories on Digital Platforms, for Public Literacy**

* Students will be introduced to various forms of presentations and platforms, including *Public Books* (<https://www.publicbooks.org/>), *The Conversation*(<https://theconversation.com/global>), *AEON* (<aeon.co>*)*, *Gushi (Story**Studio* <https://storystudio.tw/>*)*, and various new media and community newspapers. Students should refine their storytelling based on their previous humanistic investigations.
* Core readings:

* Jacob Burg, “Pedagogy of and for the Public: Imagining the Intersection of Public Humanities and Community Literacy.” *Community Literacy Journal* 14, no. 2 (2020): 130-137. ***OR***
* Jefferson Pooley, “The Impact Platform.” *Parameters* (2017).

* Tutorial exercise:

* Students will decide on the framing and strategies for publishing their work to the public and consider potential stakeholders with whom they can collaborate.

* *Week 11: Consultation Week (Attendance required)**

* *Week 12 (and 13): Group Presentation**

* Students’ projects, as initial attempts in public humanities, will serve as references or prototypes for further exploration in other modules of the program. For example, a group focusing on public health in Hong Kong might develop their presentation into a humanities research project, public art project, independent film, YouTube video, etc. The skills for different cultural formats are expected to be acquired by students in other modules of the program.

* *Individual final paper (1500 words):**

* Each student is required to write a reflection paper on the group project in which they participated. The paper should include the following sections:

* **Improvement Opportunities:** Discuss how the group project could be enhanced, including aspects such as theme, content, time management, and presentation style.
* **Alternative Approaches:** Describe other directions the project could have taken.
* **Learning from Peers:** Identify the best projects in the class and discuss the strengths that can be learned from them.
* **Future Endeavors:** Explain whether you are interested in continuing and revising this project or if you prefer to explore other topics for future projects.

* *Utilizing ChatGPT:** Describe how ChatGPT was used to assist in the writing of this paper.

courses.recommended_readings:

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Research Methods in Public Humanities
courses.description:
This course offers an overview of research methods in Public Humanities, and equips students with the essential research skills, cultural sensitivity, and critical perspectives required to navigate this dynamic landscape. Through an immersive exploratory journey on research methods, students will engage in pilot research for a collaborative hands-on project, embodying the driving ethos of this course: engagement, praxis, and reflexivity. In this endeavor, students will explore diverse qualitative and digital research methods for investigating, documenting, and sharing stories, histories, and other forms of cultural mediation for community engagement.
This course is designed to foster an interactive and dialogic learning environment that cultivates peer collaboration and exchanges of insights. Building on the conceptual foundations laid in CURE1004 Introduction to Public Humanities, this course provides students with the practical skillset needed to unlock the transformative and empowering potential of humanities-based research that nurtures and nourishes the community.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1 Do Meaningful Research: Making a Difference
Students will be introduced to the epistemology, methodology, and an overview of research methods in Public Humanities.

Week 2 Think Out of the Box: Research Topic
Students will engage with themselves, academic literature, and understand the importance of contextualization. They will also engage with their peers to start designing a community-engaged research project.

Week 3 Be Sensitive: Ethical Considerations in Doing Research
Students will develop cultural sensitivity in evaluating the ethical implications of their research project. They will work towards adopting the best practices with research ethics in mind.

Week 4 Listen! Do an Interview, Learn from Lived Stories
Students will learn the essentials of conducting interviews, focus groups, and ethnography for community engagement. They will also practise being empathetic through role-play exercises.

Week 5 Be a Treasure Hunter—Researching in the Library
Students will equip themselves with the skills needed to be resourceful researchers, making the best use of library resources for their projects.

Week 6 Be Open-minded—Gathering Insights from the Community
While survey is not a common research method in the Humanities, students will learn the fundamentals of conducting surveys to gather insights from the community they are engaging with. They will design effective survey questions.

Week 7 Be Observant & Reflexive & Imaginative
Students will explore the art of observation as a research method. They will learn techniques for conducting systematic observations to better understand the community context and dynamics relevant to their projects. Students will practice being attentive observers and taking field notes.

Week 8 Be Tech-savvy: Workshop on Data Visualisation and Digital Scholarship Research
Students will acquire the basic knowledge of digital research tools and data visualization techniques. They will lean how to use these for data collection, analysis, and visualization to support public scholarship and storytelling.

Week 9 Reading Week/ Fieldtrip
Students will join an off-campus cultural tour to engage with the city and the local community.

Week 10 Be Receptive: Consultation
Students will discuss their project proposal drafts and share any challenges they are facing with the lecturer and/or tutors. They will receive feedback to improve their proposals.

Weeks 11 & 12 Be Eloquent: Presentations
In these two weeks, students will share the proposals for their community-engaged projects, backed by concrete pilot research data. They will also exchange insights and provide constructive feedback to their peers.

Week 13 Be a Good Storyteller: Writing a Research Paper
In addition to communicating their research to broad public audiences, students will learn the essentials of writing a readable and impactful research paper.

2. Students’ comments and feedback through emails or personal meeting with the instructor will be encouraged.

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Religion and Contemporary Life
courses.description: Despite their long history, the influence of many great religions can still be recognized in our contemporary world. This course is designed to introduce students to this influence of religion in various aspects of our modern life. It will explore a variety of religious themes such as religious myth, symbol and practice, as well as current religious issues in ethics and politics. In order to illustrate the religious presence in our contemporary life, we shall examine these themes by looking at cases of films, activities and practices that can be easily seen in the world today. It is an introductory course and expects no prerequisite knowledge from students.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge outcomes

1. To become familiar with the basic themes of the field of religious studies

2. To develop an understanding of the key religious issues in the contemporary world

3. To be able to reflect on the relationship between religion and the human condition in the contemporary world

4. To be able to engage in analysis of religious phenomena, taking into account of the key themes and issues involved.

Attitude outcomes

1. To develop a critical yet sympathetic attitude in understanding religious phenomena.

2. To develop a sensitivity to religious dimension of human life.
courses.course_syllabus:
Weekly Content

Part 1: Introduction

1. Religious Studies: Interpreting the Sacred and the Human Condition

Part II: Myth, Symbol and Science

2. Symbols and Myths: Religious Themes in Films

3. Sacred Time and Space: Rituals and Festivals

4. Religion and Science: Big Issues in Debate

Part III. Practices of Body and Soul

5. Confession and Asceticism: Christian care of the soul and the body

6. Daoist Immortality and Nourishing Life

7. Living Here and Now: Buddhist Mindfulness Techniques

8. Hijab: Muslim Dress Code for Modesty and Privacy

Part IV: Sex, Gender and Environmental Ethics

9. Can a Female/ Homosexual be a Priest? The Issues of Sex and Gender

10. The End of the World? Religious Response to the Environmental Crisis

Part V: Conflict, Community and Politics

11. Jihad: Muslims’ Struggle for God: Necessarily Violent?

12. Salvation through Revolution: Christian Liberation Theological Movement

13. Ahimsa: Buddhist Non-Violence and Peace Movement

Part VI: Conclusion

14. The Future of Religion in Contemporary Cultures

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: The Study of Religion
courses.description: This course introduces the various approaches (such as philosophical, sociological, anthropological, psychological) to the study of religion, and attempts to show how, by utilizing the fruits and methods of these approaches, the history of religions can become the discipline which has religion itself as a proper object of study. This course then focuses on the study of the chief elements of religious beliefs and practices. These will include the phenomenon of religious experiences, myths, symbols, rituals, prayer and beliefs.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge outcomes

1. To become familiar with the basic vocabulary of the field of religious studies

2. To develop an understanding of the theories of religion

3. To develop an understanding of the key issues in the study of religion

4. To be able to reflect on the relationship between religion and the human condition

5. To be able to engage in a critical analysis of religious phenomena by the key approaches, taking into account of the key issues involved.

Attitude outcomes

1. To develop a critical yet sympathetic attitude in understanding religious phenomena.

2. To develop a sensitivity to religious dimension of human life.
courses.course_syllabus:
1) Course overview

2) Defining religion

3) The Field of Religious Studies

4) Sacred Power

5) Religious Experience

6) Sacred Space and Time

7) Evil and Theodicy

8) Human Existence and Destiny

9) Myth as Sacred Story

10) Ritual as Sacred Action

11) Religion and Morality

12) Institutionalized Religion

13) Conclusion

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Chinese Culture and Its Religion
courses.description:
(UGEA1450 is double-coded with CURE1122.)

The aim of this course is to help students understand the basic religious characteristics of Chinese culture and the relationship between religions and the daily life of Chinese people. It also helps students to appreciate the richness and diversity of the Chinese traditions and acquiring essential preparation for more advanced courses in the area of Chinese culture and religions. The course is divided under the familiar categories of Buddhism, Taoism, Confucianism, and Popular religion. These traditions are, by no means, discrete and mutually exclusive. It will be demonstrated that they overlap and interact with each other through a variety of confluences and conflicts. Students will also be introduced to some primary source of the Chinese traditions including both textual and fieldwork data.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken UGEC1450 or UGEA1450
courses.learning_outcomes:
(1) understand the basic religious characteristics of Chinese Culture

(2) appreciate the diversity of Chinese Culture

(3) able to have a comprehensive understanding of the essential characteristics of Chinese civilization through a cross-disciplinary approach

(4) learn to appreciate and evaluate, by way of an integrated approach, their own cultural heritage from a broad. historical, social and intellectual perspective.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1:Chinese Culture and Archaeology: Unity and Diversity of Local cultures

Week 2:Religions and Beliefs in Ancient China: Oracle Inscriptions, Han Tombs and Life after Death

Week 3:Buddhism: Its teaching and Its influences on Chinese Language

Week 4:Chan Buddhism and Chinese Arts: Paintings, Poems and Martial Arts

Week 5:Taoism and Chinese Culture: the Wisdom of *Daode jing*

Week 6:Taoist Inner Cultivation and Preservation of Lives in Chinese Culture

Week 7:Confucian Tradition and State Religion

Week 8:Neo-Confucians and Chinese religion

Week 9:Vernacular Architecture in Chinese Villages: Lineages Hall and Temples

Week 10:The Structure of ChineseVillage Societies: Gods and Ancestors

Week 11:Common beliefs in Chinese Culture: *Fengshui* and Technical Traditions

Week 12:Common beliefs in Chinese Culture: Retribution and destiny

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: World Religions

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 713

courses.description:
(UGED1400 is double-coded with CURE1123.)

This course provides an introduction to some major religious traditions in the world from a phenomenological and historical point of view. Topics include Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Hinduism, Buddhism, Confucianism, Daoism and Local religions etc.. Through the study of religion in different cultures, this course seeks to broaden students' horizons and widen their academic experience.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken UGED1400.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge Outcomes:

(1) Students will be able to understand world religions in their historical settings and contemporary contexts of modern living; globally, locally and in their many facets

* (2) Students will be able to engage in a* critical analysis of religions by the methods of the humanities and social sciences

Skill Outcomes

(1) Students will be able to develop their skills associated with data gathering and direct observation; organizing it, understanding it, and presenting it.

(2) Students will be able to develop their skills in cross-cultural understanding and intercultural literacy.

Attitude Outcomes

(1) Students will be able to develop their ability to have a critical and yet sympathetic understanding and appreciation of religious traditions especially those one does not belong

(2) Students will be able to develop their sensitivity to ultimate concern and religious dimension of human life
courses.course_syllabus:
(1) Indian Religious Traditions:

Indus valley civilization, Vedas, Upanishads, Karma-Yoga, Bhakti movements, Theravāda Buddhism

(2) Chinese Religious Traditions:

The Three teachings--Confucianism, Taoism, & Buddhism (Mahāyāna & Vajrayāna), local religions

(3) Abrahamic Faiths:

Judaism, Christianity, Islam

In addition, on Chinese religion, students are suggested to read 李亦園《宗教與神話論集》(台北：立緒文化，九八年) and on Indian religions, read *Hinduism: A Cultural Perspective* (New Jersey: Prentice-Hall, 1993) by David R. courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Study of Christianity
courses.description:
(UGED1520 is double-coded with CURE1124.)

The primary aim of this course is to provide students a broad and comprehensive understanding of the Christian faith through introducing its characteristics, traditions, history, scripture, theology and ethics. It helps students to analyse critically the various forms of Christianity and appreciate the richness of the Christian religion that exerts so great influences on human civilizations. Through interdisciplinary dialogues, this course also aims at helping students to see how the Christian faith understands and deals with problems, predicaments, political and ethical dilemmas of humanity.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge:

* To achieve proper understanding of Christianity as a global phenomenon and some of the representative manifestations of Christianity in various local contexts.
* To obtain a comprehensive knowledge of the major aspects of Christian faith, especially its views of the meaning of human life.
* To have a basic understanding of the methodology involved in the multidisciplinary study of Christianity.

Skills:

* To be able to use the analytical framework and hermeneutical tools for the academic studies of the religious phenomena related to Christianity.
* To be capable of relating what have been leant from this course to some other academic disciplines.
* To have the ability to reflect critically on one’s own cultural and / or religious tradition.

Attitudes:

* To treasure the value of human life and be sensitive to the related ethical responsibilities.
* To appreciate the diversity within particular cultural and / religious tradition.
* To become more respectful to others’ religions, worldviews and values.

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Introduction to Cultural Management
courses.description: Cultural management is frequently perceived as a new terrain both for the acknowledgement of the role of culture in driving economic and social development, and for the dissemination of managerial thought to arrange and plan cultural entities, facilities and services. This course first aims to facilitate students’ understanding of the rising discipline of cultural management, which is embedded in the transformative understanding of culture and the on-going global movement that highlights culture-led development. Second, it introduces the managerial approach to cultural assets, initiates students’ understanding of the relationship between arts management and the market system and government policies, and imparts the preliminary knowledge for the management of the arts. Third, it displays a spectrum of major issues in cultural management, such as cultural policy, cultural organizations and institutions, venue management and event programming, heritage, conservation and cultural tourism and cultural creative industries. The breadth of the discipline as indicated allows students’exposure to the basic cultural management concepts and strategies.
courses.learning_outcomes: In terms of knowledge enrichment, students are expected to achieve the understanding of the discipline of cultural management, including its objectives and values, its relationships with the transformative understanding of culture, the on-going global movement that celebrates culture-led development. Other major issues will also be covered. Students are expected to understand preliminarily the operation of art businesses and cultural organizations in connection to market values and government administrative systems. In terms of management training, students will learn practicalities such as cultural managerial principles, planning strategies and basic skills, issues, challenges and limitations specifically for the arts and cultural resources. Discussions are largely based in the local context, supplemented with overseas and Chinese cases, with an awareness of the variances across contexts.
courses.course_syllabus:
Part I. Cultural Management as a New Discipline
This part introduces the historical and disciplinary background of cultural management.

1. Understanding Culture: history and theory
The first section presents an introduction to the course. As cultural management is embedded in the discourse of culture, this section explores the concept of culture and highlights the theoretical transition from the anthropological idea of culture as values and practices to the understanding of culture as a capital and resource. The emergence of the cultural economy concept will also be discussed.

2. Understanding the Discipline of Cultural Management
This section discusses the evolution of the discipline of cultural management over the past four decades, that is, from arts management, to cultural administration and management. It introduces the objectives of the discipline; the way that this discipline has been gradually accepted and subsequently widely practiced; and its closely related neighboring disciplines. It also discusses the professional role of cultural managers; their differences from artists and creative workers are highlighted.

3. Cultural Management in the Globalization of Culture-led Development
This section examines the discipline of cultural management from the view of the global movement that utilizes culture as a driving force for development. It discusses a set of concepts that emerged in the recent decades, such as creative industries, creative classes, cultural planning, culture-led urban regeneration and creative cities. It provides the historical background for the evolution of these concepts, discusses the issues and challenges that they encounter and their application in various local contexts.

Part II. Managing the Arts
Art management is considered a traditional realm of cultural management. This part focuses on the managerial approach to the arts. Section 4 investigates the disciplinary relationship between cultural management and traditional management, and the peculiarities of art management. Sections 5 and 6 introduce preliminarily the management of two major art sectors: performing arts and visual arts. Sections 7 and 8 are concerned with cultural management in the social environment and discuss its connections to art marketing and administration.

4. Managerial Approach to the Arts
How to manage the arts? How is art management different from the traditional study of management? This section introduces the managerial approach to the arts. It explains the disciplinary relationship between cultural management and traditional management, and discusses how art management crystallizes the philosophy of both management and the arts. Art management covers four main managerial issues, namely, strategic management and planning, human resources management, finance and accounting and marketing and consumer behavior. The empirical implications of art management’s peculiarities are also discussed.

5. Introduction to Performing Arts Management
Performing arts is one of the major subjects in art management. This section introduces the concept of performing arts and its management. It discusses basic performing arts programming strategies and its relationship with theatres and other performing art venues. Some basic modes of organizing performing arts and performing art agencies are discussed.

6. Introduction to Visual Art Management
Visual art is another major subject in art management. This section introduces the concept of visual art and its management. It discusses various types of visual art spaces and agencies (e.g., museums and art galleries), introduces their similarities and differences in concepts, functions, operational modes, policies and evaluation modes.

7. Art Consumption and Art Marketing
This section focuses on the concepts of art consumption and art marketing. It discusses the determinants of artistic values and the processes of creating and diffusing artistic values in the market. It covers the issues of art consumer behaviors, market prices, cultural business branding and positioning and art sponsorship, from both the consumption-and production-oriented perspectives.

8. Cultural Administrative Structure
The government plays a significant role in cultural development. This section attempts to investigate government and public administration in cultural affairs, its administrative structure, patronage and state-based arts funding agencies. Some recent tendencies (e.g., corporatization of government entities in cultural affairs administration) will be examined withHong Kong as the major context for study. Cultural administrative structures in China, Taiwan and the U.S. as well as their institutional reforms will also be examined.

Part III. Major Issues in Cultural Management
This part introduces some major issues in the cultural management discipline, including cultural policies, cultural organizations and institutions, cultural venues and events programming, conservation, heritage and cultural tourism, as well as cultural and creative industries. It will introduce students to the concepts, operational principles and their management or planning strategies. Cases from various contexts will be studied.

9. Cultural Policies
This section introduces the definition of cultural policies and the process of cultural policy making. It presents a preliminary discussion regarding the way that cultural policies affect cultural development. Policy issues such as government subsidies, cultural industrialization tendency, government’s promotion of art values and cultural censorship will be addressed. Cultural policies from various contexts will be examined.

10. Cultural Organizations and Institutions
This section categorizes art organizations based on their basic operational mechanisms. It models the growth of firms in the cultural field and describes the sequences and stages as well as determining factors. It also discusses the governing structure of cultural organizations (e.g., the board structure and its impact on organizational missions and strategies), cultural funding and patronage, volunteer management and management control systems. A global perspective is introduced for examining the connections between local and international cultural organizations.

11. Cultural Venues and Event Programming
This section introduces the concept of cultural venues, facilities and districts as well as events planning and programming. Students will be introduced to the theoretical discussions regarding the role of cultural venues and events in cultural development and their functions in fostering local culture. It also introduces basic principles of and strategies for cultural venue planning and strategies for event programming,including cultural venue administration, public engagement, event promotion as well as public-private partnership.

12. Cultural Tourism and Heritage Management
What are cultural tourism and cultural heritage? What is their significance and inter-relationship? This section defines and discusses their roles in culture-led urban regeneration. It also introduces the basic strategies for programming cultural tourism and explains the significance and methods of heritage conservation. The second half of this section introduces an integrative approach to cultural tourism and heritage management in order to develop cultural destinations, using European, American and Australian casesfor study.

13. Cultural and Creative Industries
The last section will investigate the theory and practice of cultural and creative industries. It will examine the historical context in which the concepts of cultural and creative economy have arisen, its adoption as public policy from the UK and worldwide and its reception and development in Asia Pacific region. It will look at policies and strategies as proposed and developed by various countries in the region with particular emphasis on China, Japan, Korea, Taiwan and Singapore and evaluate the development in Hong Kong.

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Cultural Policy and Development
courses.description: The course is an introduction to the nature, functions and making of cultural policies. Making references to examples of local and international cultural polices, it studies the relationship between the making of cultural policy and the infrastructure of power, the ideological influences behind the making of cultural policy. It also studies the latest trends in cultural development and its relationship with policy-making, and the impacts on the development of arts and culture, with particular references to the local context. The course also includes a practical aspect of drafting and developing institutional policy as part of the studies in cultural management.
courses.learning_outcomes: Students are expected to learn about how cultural policy is composed, its scope and structure, and the ideological components behind it within the context of the infrastructure of power, and to understand the impact of cultural policy in shaping the development of culture. The course also intends to equip students with the skills of drafting institutional cultural policy, which is essential for the development of a cultural institution.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. General introduction

This section gives an introduction to the various definitions of cultural policies, with discussion on its impacts on cultural development and its relationship with the social and political infrastructure.

2. Culture and Power

This section investigates how political, social and economic powers affect the outcome of artistic creation, both in terms of the formal artistic languages and the modes of production and mediation.

3. Historical Background

This section uses examples from Europe and the United States to trace historically how various modes of governance were introduced and study the political expectations and social values hidden behind these policies.

4. Globalization and Cultural policy

In the 21st Century, cultural industry is recognized as an important economic and social force. Large scale cultural projects are implemented all over the world to promote cultural tourism and creative industry. This section takes a critical look at this global trend and reviews its cultural and social impacts.

5. Cultural policy in Hong Kong

This section informs students of the infrastructure for cultural development in Hong Kong, studies the history and development of the arts within the governmental structure, and traces the various stages in developing a cultural policy for Hong Kong.

6. Institutional Policy

Cultural institutions usually have their own cultural policies to set their visions and objectives for their long term development. This section studies the various types of institutional policy, with an emphasis on helping students to learn to prepare an institutional policy paper.

7. The Ecology of Culture

This section investigates some of the core cultural values in contemporary society, with emphasis on the Hong Kong context. It also introduces an ecological approach to cultural development, and reviews the success and failure of the existing cultural policies within the public and private sectors.

8. Cultural Development and Operation in China

This section studies another model in which cultural development is strongly managed by the government. Taking Shanghai as example, students will study the process of organizing exhibitions in China and ideological thinking behind this bureaucratic system.

9. Language of the Bureaucrats

In Hong Kong, the government has tremendous influence on the ways arts and cultural programmes are implemented. This section investigates how the civil servant culture and the language they apply shape the content of the policy produced, and consequently affect the course of cultural development in Hong Kong.

Case study: Culture Heritage Commission

10. West Kowloon Cultural District: Mega Cultural Project

Focusing on the development of the West Kowloon Cultural District project, this section reviews the original proposal, public responses, and the subsequent reaction of the government, with the intention to investigate the governmental modes of thinking and strategies in actualizing cultural projects.

11. Alternative Space and Community Action

As a reaction against governmental dominancy in cultural development, different types of alternative cultural institutions and groups are formed. This section investigates the potential and limitation of various types of non-government run organizations. It also studies the strategies of community groups in working with and against the government in fighting for a more nurturing cultural environment.

Case study: Hong Kong Arts Development Council, Para site Art Space

12. Cultural Policy and the Future of Cultural Development of Hong Kong

This section looks into the future cultural development of Hong Kong, its relationship with China and other regions, and discussion the problems and possibilities for positive development.

13. Review and Discussion

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Introduction to Cultural Studies
courses.description: Through examining texts, artistic forms and discursive practices that reflect and produce different cultures, students will take part in delineating the working definition(s), methodology and critical awareness of the subject. Concepts such as multiculturalism, diaspora, hybridity, globalism/localism, transnationalism will be discussed. Emphasis will be given to Hong Kong culture as a basic reference point in its contact and interaction with other cultures.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1) Knowledge Outcomes
Basic concepts in Cultural Studies
Historical developments of Cultural Studies in England and its expansion since the 1990s
Critical dimensions of the concept of culture and their relevance to everyday life cultural practices, the working of cultural production and consumption

2) Skill Outcomes
Independent academic research on new concepts
Analytical writing on cultural concepts and approaches of cultural criticism
Presenting ideas through reading and cultural analysis

3) Attitude Outcomes
Proactive in learning and research

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Introduction to Film and Screen Studies

meetings.location: Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G02

courses.description: This course focuses on the language and the basic theories of film/media. From formal, historical and theoretical approaches, students will learn how to analyze and critique the meaning behind the cinematic images; also, will learn to integrate one’s cinematic experience in terms of emotion and reason, and to develop one’s ability in film criticism writing. In the course of teaching, three aspects will be highlighted including the critical, functional and practical ones.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon successful completion of this course, students should be able to:

- identify, describe, and analyze the technical aspects of film as art.

- deeply analyze in diverse ways of critical thinking on film/media, such as the views of genre, auteurship, and spectatorship, and grasp the ways to utilize these different analytical frameworks for the writing of film criticism.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1. Introduction to course
Film:

2. Alison Klayman, Ai Weiwei Never Sorry (2012)

Readings:

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Being Human in the Age of AI: An Introduction to Digital Ethics
courses.description:
(UGED2329 is double-coded with CURE2008.)
Artificial intelligence (AI) and data science are reshaping every aspect of contemporary life—from how we work and communicate to how decisions are made about health, education, and governance. This course introduces the ethical, social, and philosophical issues that arise from these technologies. Students will examine topics such as algorithmic bias, data surveillance, automation, misinformation, and AI’s impact on human agency and creativity. Drawing on philosophical frameworks, scientific research, and real-world case studies, the course encourages students to think critically about how technological systems embody values and power structures, and how they can be governed responsibly. The course aims to develop students’ ethical reasoning, cross-disciplinary understanding, and ability to articulate informed positions on emerging technologies. Through a team-based learning approach, students engage in weekly readings, discussions, and collaborative writing exercises that cultivate both independent judgment and cooperative problem-solving. No technical background is required.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* *Students should be able to:**

1. Analyze and Apply Ethical Frameworks: Identify and apply key ethical theories (e.g., virtue ethics, deontology, utilitarianism) to evaluate the design and deployment of AI and data-driven technologies.
2. Evaluate Global Societal Impacts: Critically assess the social and political implications of algorithmic systems—such as bias, surveillance, and data colonialism—integrating diverse global and cross-cultural perspectives.
3. Construct Ethical Arguments: Formulate and defend informed, persuasive positions on the responsible governance and ethical use of emerging technologies in contemporary society.
4. Collaborate and Communicate Effectively: Work productively in teams to synthesize complex ethical ideas and communicate them clearly through collaborative writing and oral presentations.
5. Practice Critical Reflexivity: Reflect on one’s own assumptions, values, and agency as a user, designer, or citizen within an AI-mediated world.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week 1: Introduction – Value and Technology**
How do technology and human values intersect? Explore Heidegger’s and Bryson’s perspectives on technology as a cultural practice, and discuss technology’s role in shaping human existence and meaning.

* *Week 2: Foundations of AI and Data Science**
What are artificial intelligence, machine learning, and data science? Examine foundational concepts, basic algorithms, and the societal narratives that frame technological innovation.

* *Week 3: Ethical Frameworks – Virtue, Duty, and Utility**
How can ethics guide technology? Introduce virtue ethics and human flourishing, Kantian deontology, and utilitarian reasoning as frameworks for evaluating technological decisions.

* *Week 4: Data and Surveillance Capitalism**
How does data become a source of power? Investigate the political economy of data extraction, corporate surveillance, and the ethics of privacy, consent, and digital autonomy.

* *Week 5: Algorithmic Bias and Fairness**
How do biases enter algorithms? Analyze social and technical dimensions of algorithmic bias, justice, accountability, and transparency through real-world case studies.

* *Week 6: AI as a Mirror of Humanity**
In what ways does AI reflect us? Explore how machine learning systems reproduce human cognition, prejudice, and creativity, and question the limits of “artificial” intelligence.

* *Week 7: Data Colonialism and Global Inequality**
How does AI reproduce global power asymmetries? Study postcolonial and Global South critiques of data extraction, digital labor, and the ethics of global governance.

* *Week 8: Responsibility and the “Moral Gap”**
Who is accountable for AI’s actions? Examine the “responsibility gap” in autonomous systems and discuss moral agency, liability, and ethical design.

* *Week 9: Online Manipulation and Persuasive Design**
How do digital platforms shape behavior? Investigate the behavioral economics of attention, nudging, dark patterns, and the ethics of algorithmic persuasion in social media.

* *Week 10: Deepfakes, Truth, and Misinformation**
What happens when truth becomes malleable? Discuss the epistemology of misinformation, authenticity in AI-generated media, and implications for democracy and public trust.

* *Week 11: Gamification and the Algorithmic Self**
How does gamification transform communication and learning? Analyze the role of AI in shaping motivation, reward systems, and social interaction in digital environments.

* *Week 12: In-Class Essay Examination**
Students compose a critical essay applying one or more ethical frameworks to a contemporary issue in AI and data ethics. Emphasis on synthesis, argumentation, and reflection.

* *Week 13: Conclusion – Ethics and the Future of AI**
What does a responsible AI future look like? Recap key themes, revisit philosophical questions, and reflect on individual and collective responsibility in an AI-mediated society.

courses.title: Disability Studies in Public Humanities

courses.description:
This course aims to build students’ foundational understanding of Disability Studies from the perspective of Public Humanities, and to explore how it transforms critical frameworks within the arts and humanities. It will guide students to analyze representations of disability in artistic and cultural texts, revealing their political, ethical, and aesthetic dimensions, and to consider “bodily difference” as a key category for cultural critique and social justice.
Students will compare theoretical discussions of different disability models within the humanities and social sciences, developing an understanding of the multiple meanings of “disability” as both a cultural and political concept. The course also emphasizes cultivating students’ critical reading and academic writing skills—including art criticism and cultural curation—while fostering inclusive pedagogy and principles of universal design.
Balancing theory and practice, the course covers themes such as disability and design, performance art, technological development, and global biopolitics. It encourages students to reflect on how modern societies define “normalcy,” “ability,” and “humanity.” Ultimately, the course seeks to equip students with a cross-cultural and interdisciplinary vision to understand and promote the public engagement and social transformation of inclusive culture.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* *Upon successful completion of this course, students should be able to:**

1. Demonstrate a foundational understanding of various approaches of Critical Disability Studies, including its key concepts, theoretical frameworks, and its transformative impact on Art and humanities and cultural critique.

2. Analyze Critically evaluate representations of disability in artistic, literary, and cultural texts, uncovering their political, ethical, and aesthetic dimensions.

3. Understanding “disability” as a cultural, political, and interdisciplinary concept.

4. Reflect on and critique societal constructs of normalcy, ability, and humanity, analyzing how these intersect with issues of race, gender, sexuality, and class.

5. Apply critical reading, academic writing, art criticism, and cultural curation skills, while integrating inclusive pedagogical practices and principles from CDS.

6. Engage with cutting edge theories in CDS such as Crip Theory, Biopolitics, and Design Justice to explore the intersections of disability with technology, performance, and global politics.

7. Formulate strategies to foster public engagement and social transformation through inclusive culture and accessibility, balancing theory and practice.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Module 1: Disability and Humanity**

Week 1: Humanities and Disability Studies: Foundations

This week introduces the theoretical foundations of Disability Studies and its transformative impact on the humanities. We will examine how disability challenges normative assumptions of “reason,” “wholeness,” and “normalcy” embedded in humanistic discourse.

Key Questions:

• How has disability historically been treated in literature and cultural criticism?

Readings: Brueggemann (2012) Preface;

Linton (1998) Ch.1

Week 2: Various Branch of Disability Studies and CRIP GENEALOGIES

We explore diverse strands of Disability Studies, including Mad Studies and Southern perspectives, and introduce Crip Theory as a critical epistemology for studying Hong Kong disability culture. Students will analyze how these frameworks disrupt dominant narratives of ability and citizenship.

Key Questions:

• What does it mean to consider disability as a cultural and political category rather than a medical condition? What is Mad Studies and South bound disability study?

• What are the genealogies of identity of disabilities in Hong Kong? Disability studies

Readings:

Dauncey, Sarah. |introduction”, Disability in contemporary China: Citizenship, identity and culture. Cambridge University Press, 2020.

Lo, Clayton. "Crip Genealogies and Disability Citizenship in the Sinophone World: A Transnational Cultural Epistemology." Including Disability 6 (2025).

Week 3: Academic Ableism: Barriers in Higher Education

This lesson extend the concept of disability right and citizenship, to interrogates the structural ableism within neoliberal academia, focusing on rhetorical and institutional barriers faced by disabled scholars. We will discuss how mental disability intersects with academic norms.

Key Questions:

• How do universities reproduce ableist ideologies?

• What strategies can dismantle these barriers?

Readings:

Dolmage, Jay T. |Introduction” Academic ableism: Disability and higher education. University of Michigan Press, 2017.

Margaret Price, “Introduction|, Mad at School: Rhetorics of Mental Disability and Academic Life (Ann Arbor: University of Michigan Press, 2011), 25–33.

* *Module**2 Disability and Art

Week 4: Representation of Disabilities in History

We examine how traditional literature and philosophy uses disability as a narrative device and consider the ethical implications of such representations. Students will learn to critique the cultural texts that employ disability as metaphor and explore alternative modes of storytelling.

Key Questions:

• What is “narrative prosthesis”?

• How can disability narratives resist symbolic reduction?

Reading:

Mitchell, David T, and Sharon L. Snyder. “chapter 2”, Narrative prosthesis: Disability and the dependencies of discourse. University of Michigan Press, 2014.

Reference:

李智良: 《房間》, 2008.

Rashed, Mohammed Abouelleil. "Mad pride and the creation of culture." Royal Institute of Philosophy Supplements 94 (2023): 201-217.

Week 5: Western Visual Culture, Disability Aesthetics and International Inclusive Art

This week focuses on visual culture and the politics of staring. We will analyze how disability aesthetics challenge normative bodily beauty standards and redefine artistic value for the artist with disability.

Key Questions:

• How does visual rhetoric construct “normal” and “abnormal”?

• What does disability aesthetics contribute to art and literary theory?

Reading:

Siebers, Tobin. “Introduction” Disability aesthetics. Vol. 2. Ann Arbor: University of Michigan Press, 2010.

Reference:

Zhuang, Kuansong Victor. "The included: Disability-led arts within inclusion in Singapore." Journal of Literary & Cultural Disability Studies 15.4 (2021): 471-487.

SEMARIE, RO. "The politics of Staring: Visual Rhetorics of disability in Popular Photography." Reading 4 (2002): 3.

Week 6: Queer/Crip Performance, Body Politics, and Public Space

We explore intersections of gender, sexuality, and disability through performance studies. Students will analyze how crip and queer identities disrupt normative scripts of embodiment.

Key Questions:

• How do performance and language subvert ableism?

Crip theory

• How the case of 夬兒藝術駐留(@crip.art.residency.emerged in hk?

Readings:

Sandahl, Carrie, and Philip Auslander. "Introduction: Disability studies in commotion with performance studies." Bodies in commotion: Disability and performance (2005): 1-12.

Reference:

Sandahl, Carrie. "Queering the crip or cripping the queer?: Intersections of queer and crip identities in solo autobiographical performance." GLQ: a journal of lesbian and gay studies 9, no. 1 (2003): 25-56.

* *Module**3 Disability and Technology

Week 7: Design Meet Disability

This session examines the historical and contemporary relationship between design and disability. We will discuss accessibility in digital humanities and the politics of technological affordances.

Key Questions:

• How does design shape disability experience? Why Disability was highly relate to the design issue in human history?

• What is affordance theory in Digital Humanities study?

Reading:

Achiam, Marianne, Michael May, and Martha Marandino. "Affordances and distributed cognition in museum exhibitions." Museum Management and curatorship 29, no. 5 (2014): 461-481.

Week 8: Curation of Accessibility in the Public Culture

We analyze universal design as a political project, examining how accessibility moves beyond technical compliance to become a mechanism of international human rights governance. This session explores how museums and cultural institutions negotiate accessibility standards, often under global frameworks that frame inclusion as a normative obligation. Students will critically assess whether these practices foster genuine equity or reproduce tokenistic approaches.

Key Questions:

• How does universal design operate as a global governance tool?

• What tensions arise between accessibility as a right and as a branding strategy?

• How do cultural institutions balance ethics, aesthetics, and compliance?

Reading:

Hamraie, Aimi. |Sloped Technoscience Curb Cuts, Critical Frictions, and Disability (Maker) Cultures|, Building access: Universal design and the politics of disability. U of Minnesota Press, 2017, 97-130.

Johnson, Megan A., Eliza Chandler, Chelsea Temple Jones, and Lisa East. "Crip Digital Intimacies: The Social Dynamics of Creating Access through Digital Technology." Societies 14, no. 9 (2024): 174.

Reference:

Zhuang, Kuansong Victor, and Gerard Goggin. "New possibilities or problems for disability and inclusion? The case of AI and ADMs across work." Telematics and Informatics 92 (2024): 102156.

Week 9: Intersectionality and Design Justice

This week introduces design justice as a politicized framework for community-led accessibility practices. Students will reflect on how design can foster liberation rather than reinforce oppression.

Key Questions:

• How does accessibility intersect with race, gender, and class instead of disabilities?

• What is the reflecsive curation in which allows the accessibility technology contributes to community development and participation?

Reading:

Lazard, Carolyn. Accessibility in the Arts: A Promise and a Practice. Recess Art, 2019.

Costanza-Chock, Sasha. |Design Values: Hard-Coding Liberation?|, Design justice: Community-led practices to build the worlds we need. The MIT Press, 2020, 32-42.

* *Module**4: Disability and Global Biopolitics

Week 10: Biopolitics, Vulnerability and Debility

In this section, we further examine the theoretical discussion of biopolitical regimes that produce vulnerability and debility, focusing on intersectional feminist instead of merely critical disability perspectives.

Key Questions:

• How do the states and the inter-state power manage the life and death of vulnerable population?

• What is the biopolitical relationship between care, control and regulation of gender violance?

Reading:

Wearing, Sadie, Yasmin Gunaratnam, and Irene Gedalof. "Introduction Frailty and Debility." Feminist Review 111, no. 1 (2015): 1-9.Week 12: Disability Justice and Crip

Hosbach, Ian. "Book Review: Jasbir K. Puar's The Right to Maim: Debility, Capacity, Disability." Review of Disability Studies: An International Journal 17, no. 2 (2021).

Week 11: Rehabilitative Post-socialism and Chrono-biopolitics of Disable Citizenship

This lesson explores how disability intersects with race, gender, and sexuality in postsocialist contexts. We will discuss curative violence and temporal politics of rehabilitation, as non-western form of chronobiopolitics of disability.

Key Questions:

• How does postsocialism shape disability narratives?

• What is “curative violence”?

Reading:

Kolárová, Katerina. |Introduction)Rehabilitative Postsocialism: Disability, Sex, and Race in Eastern Europe. University of Michigan Press, 2025.

Kim, Eunjung.”|How to Inhabit the Time Machine with Disability” Curative violence: Rehabilitating disability, gender, and sexuality in modern Korea. Duke University Press, 2017.

Week 12: Access Washing, Disability Justice and Decolonizing Disability

We conclude by interrogating “access washing”—the superficial adoption of accessibility measures without structural change. Students will engage with Disability Justice principles and decolonial critiques of global disability politics.

Key Questions:

• What is access washing and why is it problematic?

• How does Disability Justice differ from inclusion?Access washing

Reading:

Puar, Jasbir K. "4. CRITICAL DISABILITY STUDIES AND THE QUESTION OF PALESTINE: Toward Decolonizing Disability." In Crip genealogies, pp. 117-134. Duke University Press, 2023.

Jaffee, Laura. "Access Washing at the Imperial University: Militarism, Occupation, and Struggles Toward Disability Justice." (2020).

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Feminisms: Thoughts and Movements
courses.description:
(UGEC2273 is double-coded with CURE2022.)

A long-lasting struggle of gender equity was triggered by the women’s movements in England and U.S in the end of 19th century. Feminists fight for equality by publications, demonstrations and organizations. In 20th century, the feminist movement matured. Feminists have been more sensitive to the differences among women of different race, class, culture, religion and circumstances and noticed the importance of differences in unity. This course will introduce the strategies and main arguments of various kinds of feminism. Through learning from others’ experience, history and our daily life, we will understand the development of diversity in feminisms. We can have more understanding of feminists fighting for the right of disadvantage groups by self-review and mutual criticism in different times and culture.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. 解釋女權運動的歷史脈胳

2. 比較女權主義各種流派的生成與時代變遷之關係

3. 分析近代與現今性別權益事件

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Hong Kong Cinema
courses.description:
(UGEC2241 is double-coded with CURE2030.)

This course is a survey of Hong Kong cinema, with emphasis on its contemporary development. This course will cover both textual and contextual analyses: we treat Hong Kong cinema as an art form and introduce different ways to appreciate them and criticize them; we also read these films as commercial cultural products to investigate links between movies and society at-large. The course will introduce basic film theories and film languages. It helps students to reflect on their own relationship with these Hong Kong movies they grow up with, as well as help elucidate the complex relationship between Hong Kong cinema and Hong Kong identity.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
CURE2030 is double-coded with UGEC2241.
Not for students who have taken UGEC2241 or 2774.
courses.learning_outcomes: Students are expected to acquire a general understanding of basic film languages, and to develop basic skill in film appreciation and film criticism. After the course students should be able to understand Hong Kong history and Hong Kong society from the perspective of cinema, with critical awareness of related issues pertaining to gender, class, and cultural identity. They are also expected to understand the intimate relationship between film production and film consumption, as well as the differences and connections between art and commerce in the domain of Hong Kong cinema.
courses.course_syllabus:
The lectures are organized historically, allowing students to gain a full picture of not only Hong Kong cinema’s history but also the history of Hong Kong. Students will also learn the basic techniques of film appreciation and film criticism through the various examples introduced. Lectures are composed of lecturer’s presentation and close analysis of selected film clips, through which students will be called attentions to specific stylistic, thematic, and theoretical issues. Combining the lectures and film analysis, students will learn both specific issues about Hong Kong cinema and acquire basic training of film analysis.

| | Emilie Yueh-yu Yeh and Darrell William Davis, “Warning! Category III: The Other Hong Kong Cinema” *Film Quarterly* 54.4 (Summer 2001): 12-26. |

| **10.****Contemporary Commercial Cinema (II)** | |
| Required Reading: | David Bordwell, *Planet Hong Kong, Ch. 8* |
| Optional Reading: | 羅卡:〈吳宇森與林嶺東比較〉(HKIFF, 1997) |
| | 何思穎:〈終極犯禁〉(張偉雄 2002) |
| | Tony Williams, “Space, Place, and Spectacle: The Crisis Cinema of John Woo” (Fu & Desser 2000) |
| | |
| **11.****Films about 1997: Death and Identity Crisis** | |
| Required Reading: | 石琪:〈八十年代香港電影的成就感和危機感〉(HKIFF, 1991) |
| Optional Reading: | Kwai-cheung Lo, “Transnationalization of the Local in Hong Kong Cinema” (Yau 2001) |
| | Natalia Chan Sui Hung, “Rewriting History: Hong Kong Nostalgia Cinema and Its Social Practice” (Fu & Desser 2000) |
| | |
| **12.****Hong Kong Cinema and Hollywood** | |
| Required Reading: | Laikwan Pang “Copying *Kill Bill*” *Social Text* 83 |
| Optional Reading: | David Williams: “Enter the Dragon: Interview with Ang Lee” *American Cinematographer* 82.1 |
| | |
| **13.****Films after 1997: Fruit Chan and Milkyway Image** | |
| Required Reading: | 彭麗君：〈銀河影象電影世界中的男性關係網〉《中外文學》 29.10 (March 2001) |
| Optional Reading: | 洛楓《盛世邊緣》第五章。 |
| | |
| **14.****Conclusion** | |

William M W Mong Eng Bldg 706

courses.description:
(UGEC2294 is double-coded with CURE2042.)

The course introduces the student a basic knowledge of Xiqu (Chinese Opera), including its artistic features as a performing art, and the characteristics of different kinds of local Xiqu, such as Beijing Opera, Kunqu, Shanghai Opera and Cantonese Opera. Based on the basic knowledge of the art-form, we will discuss the cultural issues reflecting in the plays and performances. Through the textual analysis of scripts and performances, students will be introduced different topics of Xiqu related to cultural studies.
courses.learning_outcomes:
一、學生對戲曲藝術和歷史的基本掌握

二、介紹當代重要戲曲研究成果

三、啟發同學思考戲曲藝術與當代文化的關係
courses.course_syllabus:
一、戲曲史初識

1. 戲曲與宗教的緊密關係、宋明期間戲曲表演模式的建立(第一週)

二、戲曲表演特色

1. 唱、唸、做、打 (象徵與虛擬：程式性的溝通方法) (第二週)

2. 形體‧空間‧時間 (第三週)

三、戲曲與戲臺

1. 歷代戲臺演變；戲臺與觀眾的關係 (從鄉村到城市) (第四週)

2. 神功戲 (第五週)

四、地方戲曲

1. 當京劇成了「中國戲曲」(第六週)

2. 崑曲：文人雅士的情慾搬演 (第七週)

3. 從越劇到黃梅調的上海中國與臺灣華文化 (第八週)

五、表演中國

1. 漢室與蠻夷：從戲服、面譜到劇目 (第九週)

2. 實驗戲曲中的想象中國：進念的實驗戲曲 (第十週)

六、香港電影中的粵劇

1. 香港戲曲電影中的新經典與半唐番 (第十一週)

2. 九七前後香港電影的粵劇再現 (第十二週)

七、戲曲與性別

1. 論「反串」(第十三週)

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Understanding Comic Arts
courses.description:
(UGED2326 is double-coded with CURE2051.)

Nowadays comic strip has become one of the major forms of visual narratives in our culture. Both comics and film are visual arts originally developed within the period of late 19th century and early 20th century. Although film has long ago established its artistic legitimacy, comic art still struggle for recognition. However, as Will Eisner put it, comic strip is a “Sequential Art” and has its own unique aesthetic nature. This course tries to inquire the governing aesthetic principles of comics and investigate its unique method of expression. We will also try to delineate the special communication mode of comics and its relationship with different aspects of modern society and culture. Students will learn to appreciate comic art from different perspectives, and strengthen analytical skills for visual media.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Through the lectures, students will recognize the aesthetic nature of sequential art and locate the humanistic values and meanings of comic arts for modern society and culture.

2. Through tutorial discussions, student will apply the analytical skills and formal methods to interpret comic and visual texts.

3. Through mid-term assignment (research portfolio on the visual representation of comics), student will distinguish, compare, analyze and criticize different visual and multimedia sources.

4. Through final paper, student will prepare and manage to do an academic research on visual representation.

5. Student will aware, judge and appraise the practical values of the analytical skills and historical knowledge provided by this course after they complete the mid-term assignment and final paper.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Section 1: The Rise of Comic Art**

1. Comics as a Form of Reading

·Will Eisner, “Comics as a Form of Reading,” *Comics & Sequential Art*. Tamarac: Poorhouse Press, 1985.

·Scott McCloud, “Setting the Record Straight,” *Understanding Comics: The Invisible Art*. New York: HarperPerennial, 1994.

·Edward Fuchs（章國鋒譯）：〈導論〉。《歐洲漫畫史（古代－1848年）》。上海：上海人民出版社，2008。

2. The Rise of Comics and Cartoon

·周蘭平編著：〈動漫的歷史軌蹟〉。《動漫的歷史》。重慶：重慶出版社，2007。

·周蘭平編著：〈世界動漫50年〉。《動漫的歷史》。重慶：重慶出版社，2007。

·一可、未名、王軍編著：〈游弋在歷史長河之上〉。《小人書的歷史：漫談中國連環畫百年興衰》。重慶：重慶出版社，2008。

·Nick Meglin（張敢、吳菁菁譯）：〈幽默的連環漫畫〉。《當代西方14位頂級幽默畫家幽默插畫》。北京：中國青年出版社，2009。

* *Section 2: Decoding the Basic Elements of Comic Art**

3. Image as Narrative Tools

·Will Eisner, “Imagery,” *Comics & Sequential Art*. Tamarac: Poorhouse Press, 1985.

·Will Eisner, “Image as Narrative Tools,” *Graphic Storytelling and Visual Narrative*. New York & London: W. W. Norton & Company, 2008.

·Scott McCloud, “Writing with Pictures,” *Making Comics*. New York: Harper, 2006.

4. Time Frames

·Will Eisner, “Timing” & “The Frame,” *Comics & Sequential Art*. Tamarac: Poorhouse Press, 1985.

·Scott McCloud, “Blood in the Gutter” & “Time Frames,” *Understanding Comics: The Invisible Art*. New York: HarperPerennial, 1994.

5. Meanings of “Lines”

·Scott McCloud, “Living in Line” & “The Vocabulary of Comics,” *Understanding Comics: The Invisible Art*. New York: HarperPerennial, 1994.

6. Words, Writing and Sequential Art

·Will Eisner, “Writing & Sequential Art,” *Comics & Sequential Art*. Tamarac: Poorhouse Press, 1985.

·Scott McCloud, “Show and Tell,” *Understanding Comics: The Invisible Art*. New York: HarperPerennial, 1994.

·Scott McCloud, “The Power of Words,” *Making Comics*. New York: Harper, 2006.

7. Facial Expression and Body Language

·Will Eisner, “Expressive Anatomy,” *Comics & Sequential Art*. Tamarac: Poorhouse Press, 1985.

·Scott McCloud, “Stories for Humans,” *Making Comics*. New York: Harper, 2006.

8. Sense of Place and Perspective

·Scott McCloud, “World Building,” *Making Comics*. New York: Harper, 2006.

·王穎：〈氛圍設計〉；〈鏡頭設計〉。《漫畫設計教程》。上海：上海人民美術出版社，2007。

* *Section 3: Reception, Fan Culture and Cultural Economy of Comic Art**

8. Narrator and Reader in Comic Narrative

·Will Eisner, “All Kinds of Stories” & “Storytellers,” *Graphic Storytelling and Visual Narrative*. New York & London: W. W. Norton & Company, 2008.

·Will Eisner, “The Reader” & “Reader Influences,” *Graphic Storytelling and Visual Narrative*. New York & London: W. W. Norton & Company, 2008.

·蕭湘文〈漫畫中的意識型態〉。《漫畫研究──傳播觀點的檢視》。台北市：五南圖書出版股份有限公司，2002。

9. Comic Fan and the Comic Industry

·蕭湘文〈漫畫讀者〉；〈漫畫媒介的市場經濟〉；〈現今的漫畫產業〉。《漫畫研究──傳播觀點的檢視》。台北市：五南圖書出版股份有限公司，2002。

·John Fiske, “The Cultural Economy of Fandom,” in Lisa Lewis, ed. The Adoring Audience, Routledge, 1992.

* *Section 4: Comics and Modern Society**

10. Graphic Book and the Discovery of “the Child”

·柄谷行人（趙京華譯）：〈兒童之發現〉。《日本現代文學的起源》。北京：生活‧讀書‧新知三聯書店，2003。

·林真美：〈繪本的誕生〉。《繪本之眼》。台北市：天下雜誌出版，2010。

·松居直（郭雯霞、徐小潔譯）：〈孩子的成長與圖畫書〉；〈通過兒童看圖畫書〉；〈兒童本位的圖畫書〉。《我的圖畫書論》。上海：上海人民美術出版社，2009。

11. Comics and Modern Visual Culture

·**J. Hillis Miller, “**Word and Image,” ***Illustration*****.** Cambridge, Mass.: Harvard University Press, 1992.

·Nick Meglin（張敢、吳菁菁譯）：〈兒童圖書〉。《當代西方14位頂級幽默畫家幽默插畫》。北京：中國青年出版社，2009。

·郭書瑄：〈從布雷克到夏卡爾──插畫藝術的大實驗時代〉；〈風格，風格，還是風格〉。《插畫考》。台北市：如果出版社，2007。

12. Comics, Animation and Modern Politics

·洪長泰：〈豐子愷抗戰漫畫中的戰爭與和平〉；〈現代藝術與政治──廖冰兄漫畫中的困局〉。《新文化史與中國政治》。台北：一方出版社，2003。

·David Vernal, “War and Peace in Japanese Science Fiction Animation,” *Animation Journal* (Fall, 1995)

13. Comics and the Cultural Imagination of Modern Science

·武田雅哉（任鈞華譯）：〈機械與科學的狂想曲〉；〈愛與魂的科學革命〉。《飛翔吧！大清帝國──近代中國的幻想科學》。台北市：遠流出版事業股份有限公司，2008。

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Foundation of Visual Design
courses.description: This practical class consists of lectures, readings, hands-on visual projects, and workshops, in which two main software will be taught: Adobe Photoshop and Adobe Premiere. Students will examine visual experiences from the perspectives of creators and viewers and will learn how to design images, edit videos, and add primary visual effects for their projects. At the same time, the practical work with these tools will help us better understand how they are used in society - the modes of thinking and inquiry they enable, their strengths and weaknesses.
courses.learning_outcomes:
- This course covers the basics of visual effects and post-production; students can use the tools as a creative medium in the context of contemporary art.

- Apply essential skills and concepts in the development of critical and innovative design outcomes.

- A broad overview of state of the art in new media art, critical design, and media theory.

- Students will gain experiences from technologies and methods related to visual cultures

and aesthetic functions in learning design.

- Develop students’ aesthetic sensitivity and the ability of visual aesthetic judgment. - Enhance students’ portfolio for job seeking.

Week 5

Week 6

* *2. Premiere Practice II**

(1) Create and Open Projects

(3) Edit Tracks in the Timeline

Week 7

Week 8

To finish a short drama film (< 5min) by using the footage provided in 2hours

Week 11

Week 13

(1) Software, hardware, and social media

(2) Media after software

(3) Software epistemology

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Myth, Symbol and Ritual
courses.description: This course deals with the inter-relationship of myth, symbol and ritual, which are believed to play important part in human religious configuration. The human meaning of myth, symbol and ritual will be recovered in the context of social, cultural and psychological effects upon their believers. Meanwhile, theories and historical examples will be emphasized.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge outcomes
Upon successful completion of the course, students should be able to understand:
(1) the role of myth, symbol and ritual in the human religious configuration;
(2) the various theoretical approaches to the study of religions and their social, cultural and psychological effects upon their believers; and
(3) the way religious symbol, myth and ritual interact with society: how they operate within, and are produced by society.

Attitude outcomes
Upon successful completion of the course, students are expected:
(1) to understand how myth, symbol, and ritual operate, consciously or unconsciously, in our daily life;
(2) to develop a critical approach to examine the various components embodied in religious faiths;
(3) to be able to cross disciplinary boundaries with an analytical mind; and
(4) to be able to integrate theory and practice.
courses.course_syllabus:
Course Schedule:
1) Introduction
2) Theoretical approach (I): Myth, folktale, and history
3) Theoretical approach (II): Symbol, symbolism and religious language
4) Theoretical approach (III): Ritual theory
5) Fieldtrip
6) Ethnographic case study (I): Greek
7) Ethnographic case study (II): Hebrew
8) Ethnographic case study (III) Chinese, Japan
9) Fieldtrip
10) Topical Study: The notion of hell and salvation ritual for the dead in China
11) Film watching and discussion
12) Contextual approach (I): Myth, ritual, social reality
13) Contextual approach (II): Myth, ritual and gender
