courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Media Arts Management
courses.description: This course introduces a new genre in the art discipline that employs electronic and digital devices as the main medium of communication. The course contains three sessions. The first session explains the birth of this set of contemporary art practices in relation to the Dadaist movement of the early twentieth century that opened up new concepts for visual arts. This will be followed by a session that discusses the range of media now commonly adopted in the discipline, including video, photography, computer graphics, webpage, and the Internet. Different set of techniques are involved in these productions, and they often generate quite different social impacts that affect both the producers and the users. Students are instructed to understand both the technological and social aspects of these areas. The last session comprises a series of workshops that lead students to appreciate and comment on the most featured productions. Emphasis is placed on the angle to promote this new art type as a rising cultural sector in the society.
courses.learning_outcomes: Upon completion of the course, students will be able to describe and define the technical terms employed in the new media productions; to acquire the basic techniques involved in shooting, controlling sound effect, and designing for interactive visual experience; to identify the software and skills essential for creating different virtual experience; to recognize the economic aspects of using multi-media in cultural sectors; to analyze the social coverage and impacts of using the Internet for distribution; and to develop independent thinking about the cyber-culture and artificial identity.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Art and Technology

The history of new media art began in the early 20th century. The well-read article by Walter Benjamin defines a new age in art distribution. This lecture discusses the adoption of technological devices in modern art production, as well as the impact factors of using technology that both contribute to and alter the original aims of traditional arts.

2. How ‘new’ is the New Media?

How does ‘New Media’ differ from the old one? Why was it born? This lecture discusses the work by Dali and Duchamp, as well as their critiques. It looks at how the artists found interests in the new devices and how they employed so in expressing thoughts and feelings during the so-called Dadaist and Pop Movement.

3. Virtual Effects and Visual Culture

New technologies gave visual culture a new life in the 20th century that opened up many different opportunities of making interaction through the use of prints, drawings, photographs, and videos. This lecture discusses the concepts of simulation and immersion in visual arts. It also introduces the concept of virtual reality, which has been widely applied in both two and three-dimensional work.

4. Films and Animations

This lecture continues the last one in discussing the idea of realism and representation in media production. This one focuses on the images reproduced in films and video games. It discusses the theories employed in photography and filming with digital devices that lead students to think how the new technologies alter the traditional views about representation.

5. Economy and the Web Media

The New Media is often regarded as being reasonably economical in operation, compared with other traditional means of presentation. This lecture discusses the economic aspects of using different web engines and forums, as well as the commercial limits in employing those devices.

6. Wikis and Web 2.0

Web 2.0 is a concept introduced at the beginning of this century to describe the combination of software (HTML), hardware, and Internet in forming a major sector of social interaction. The Wikipedia introduced in 2004 gave every user a new participant identity in the virtual world. This lecture discusses how the boom of Internet information alter social practices and ideas.

7. Games, Animation And the Cyber-world

In countries, such as Japan and Korea, games and animation form a major sector in the creative industries. This lecture introduces the Dreamweaver and Power Game Factory. It discusses the social impacts of those new entertaining devices in re-defining the issues of gender, ethnics, and even national identities.

8. Workshop I: Installation and Contemporary Arts

In this workshop session, the instructor will lead students to look at the classic examples of installation in contemporary art display. The work by Damien Hurst, Cai Guoqiang, Xu Bing, and other contemporary artists will be discussed.

9. Workshop II: Editing Techniques in Photography

This workshop leads students to practice photography and understand the central theories in shooting in relation to previous lecture on visual culture.

10. Workshop III: Editing Techniques in Filming

This workshop teaches students the skills in editing and handling effects with Final Cut Pro and Snapz.

11. Workshop III: Sound, music and noise

This workshop introduces the Sound Studio Pro, and leads students to acquire the skills in audio capture and sound recording.

12. Workshop IV: Webpage, Internet and Heritage

This workshop reviews the use of web technology in preserving and reconstructing heritage and historical sites.

13. Project presentation

14. Conclusion: Can the New Media be Forever ‘New’?

This last lecture is a response to the question stated in the first lecture, and is the key question in the field of New Media Studies. This lecture discusses how the new media constructs an artificial identity in the expanded visual world based on technology.

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Design Your Exhibition – Curatorial Planning and Practice
courses.description:
“Curator” once referred to an expert in keeping collections and making exhibitions, but has been expanded to encompass a hybrid role in innovation and knowledge creation. Today, curators are active agents in social and cultural transformation, taking part in dynamic projects presented by museums, commercial galleries, non-profit art spaces, biennials, art fairs, and many disruptive platforms beyond the field of art that didn’t previously exist. Curators produce knowledge, engage their audience, spark innovation, and constantly challenge the role of “curator” beyond the confines of the status quo across varied institutional settings.

This course aims to provide students with hands-on experience in exhibition curation and engages students in a broad spectrum of real-world experiences and professional networking opportunities, aiming to prepare students for future curatorial projects/jobs. It covers topics such as idea conceptualization, proposal writing, pitching, budgeting, contracting, publication, exhibition design, installation and exhibition maintenance. By co-curating an exhibition in a non-profit art space in Hong Kong, students will take part in the various stages of exhibition making. With a focus on exhibition experience, audience engagement, and sociability, this course will train students in the role of being a curator and enable students to develop leadership skills and critically apply knowledge conducive to creative outcomes.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
1. Pre-requisite: CUMT2007 Curation in Arts and Culture or CURE2407 Curation in Arts and Culture
2. Not for students who have taken CUMT3005.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completing the course, students will be able to:
• Explain the key roles of being a curator
• Develop skills in the various stages of exhibition making from a broad spectrum of real-world experience and professional networking opportunities
• Acquire personal and social competencies to work cooperatively in a team
• Develop critical analysis skills concerning exhibition experiences, audience engagement and sociability
• Develop an attitude of innovation and creativity by challenging assumptions in the ever-expanding field of curation

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Interpretation of Heritage

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 706
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 706
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 706
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 706

courses.description:
Cultural heritage is a dynamic and evolving concept that encompasses various interpretations rather than being a fixed notion. It includes a wide range of perspectives, from Eurocentric to post-colonial, with an increasing focus on living culture that intertwines global and local narratives. This field involves diverse interpretive lenses, public discourse, human rights issues, and a shift from mere observation to immersive experiences. The interpretations of cultural heritage are shaped by numerous stakeholders, including educators, public program participants, researchers, writers, designers, creatives, consultants, curators, scientists, and historians. In recent years, technological advancements have further enhanced the use of digital media and social networking in cultural heritage, fostering new connections between visitors and heritage. Consequently, the interpretation of cultural heritage remains dynamic and fluid, embracing a variety of approaches and philosophies.
This course aims to provide students with a broad knowledge of cultural heritage interpretations and the theoretical frameworks that offer different lenses and approaches for thinking about cultural heritage. It covers various types of cultural heritage, the role of storytelling in heritage interpretation, heritage as public discourse, identity issues, visitor engagement and education, tourism, community involvement, and ethical issues. Additionally, this course will examine how technology plays a role in engaging audiences and facilitating interpretations. By offering students diverse concepts of cultural heritage interpretations, they will gain a comprehensive understanding of current interpretive approaches, emerging trends, and innovations in the interpretation of heritage.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken CUMT3009 or CUMT4101 or CURE4411.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Explore key approaches, philosophies, and theories of cultural heritage interpretation.
* Understand cultural heritage as an evolving concept, encompassing various types of heritage, the role of storytelling, public discourse, identity issues, visitor engagement, tourism, community involvement, and ethical considerations.
* Examine critical issues related to current interpretive approaches, emerging trends, and innovations in heritage interpretation.
* Develop skills to critically interpret cultural heritage and its relationship with technology.
* Cultivate an attitude of innovation and creativity by challenging assumptions within existing approaches to cultural heritage interpretation.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *1. Types of Cultural Heritage**

* + Tangible and intangible heritage
+ Post-colonialism

* *2. The Role of Storytelling in Heritage Interpretation**

* + Techniques for effective storytelling
+ Crafting narratives that engage and educate

* *3. Cultural Inclusivity and Heritage as Public Discourse**

* + Approaching diverse cultural contexts with respect
+ Strategies for inclusive interpretation
+ The role of heritage in public conversations and identity

* *4. Interpretive Planning and Design**

* + Steps in creating an interpretive plan
+ Techniques for interpreting traditions, rituals, and oral histories
+ Preserving and presenting intangible heritage

* *5. Heritage and Identity**

* + Exploring the connection between cultural heritage and identity

* *6. Technology in Heritage Interpretation**

* + Use of digital tools and media
+ Virtual reality, augmented reality, and interactive exhibits
+ Social media and online communities

* *7. Visitor Engagement and Education**

* + Techniques to engage different audience groups
+ Transforming passive observation into immersive experiences
+ Educational programs and resources for schools and educators

* *8.** **Heritage Tourism**

* + The relationship between heritage sites and tourism
+ Sustainable tourism practices

* *9. Museum and Exhibition Interpretation**

* + Best practices for museum displays and exhibitions
+ Visitor-centered approaches
+ Using senses beyond sight to create immersive experiences

* *10. Community Involvement in Heritage Interpretation**

* + Strategies for involving local communities
+ Case studies of community-led interpretation projects

* *11. Ethical Issues in Heritage Interpretation**

* + Addressing ethical dilemmas and controversies
+ Balancing accuracy with sensitivity

* *12. Future Trends in Cultural Heritage Interpretation**

* + Emerging trends and innovations
+ The future of heritage interpretation
courses.feedback_evaluation: Course and Teaching Evaluation Questionnaire
courses.required_readings:
* *1. Types of Cultural Heritage**

* Winter, Tim. *Post-Conflict Heritage, Postcolonial Tourism*. Routledge, 8 Nov. 2007.
* Campelo, Adriana, et al. *Cultural Heritage*. Routledge, 15 Nov. 2018.

* *2. The Role of Storytelling in Heritage Interpretation**

* Staiff, Russell. *Re-Imagining Heritage Interpretation: Enchanting the Past-Future*. Farnham, Surrey, England; Burlington, VT, Ashgate Publishing, 2014.
* Goddard, Jennifer. *Construed Heritage*. Lexington Books, 9 Nov. 2020.

* *3. Cultural Inclusivity, Heritage as Public Discourse**

* Ashworth, Gregory, et al. *Pluralising Pasts*. Pluto Press (UK), 20 Oct. 2007.
* Guardado, Martin. *Discourse, Ideology and Heritage Language Socialization: Micro and Macro Perspectives*. De Gruyter, 2018.

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Museum Studies

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 706

courses.description:
Museums have multiple roles in a society. They are academic institutes and public arenas for education. In the broad term, they represent national culture and communal heritage. This course leads students to understand museum exhibitions and management within social contexts. The lectures respond to the following issues:
1. What are the functions and achievements of museum activities?
2. In what ways can we understand and learn from an exhibition? What are the essential elements of museum catalogues and cultural writing?
3. How do museum workers balance both marketing and social needs?
4. In what ways can we participate in the operation of a museum?
The course draws discussions from both the perspectives of museum workers and visitors. Some current issues concerning the roles of museums are also covered.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken CUMT3108 or ANTH4720 or HIST5514
courses.course_syllabus:
1 Introduction:What is a museum?
The introduction gives an overview of the museum structure and definition. This will introduce the various types of museums established around the world, defined according to the range of their collections. This will also compare the organization structure of a museum, in which the curatorial teams are working with a range of specialists relevant.

2 The history of museums
The ‘history’ of museum sounds alien to the context of museum studies. But museum workers are coming to beware that the museums are products of modernity that marked a distinctive times in which antiquities could be treated as shared, public properties. This lecture reviews the establishments of the Louvre and the British Museum in the 18th century; and discusses how ‘museums’ were contextualized first in Japan and later on in China.

3 Curatorship I: Exhibiting Authenticity and Interpretation
As the last lecture recalls, ‘Museum as Ritual’ has become an established social understanding—museum is a place where esteemed objects are being exhibited and therefore recognized. The first problem that encounters all museum works is whether the objects they are showing, or the theme of the objects that they are presenting are ‘authentic’. In this lecture, we will look at examples of some early colonial museums and study the created themes that they presented.

4 Curatorship II: Organizing a thematic exhibition
Putting up a thematic exhibition (alongside the permanent exhibition) is a common practice in most museums. This lecture will give instructions as to what elements a thematic show should include. Then students are organized into groups to do a role play session for three parties: museum workers, overseas collaborators, and the donors. From this exercise, students will learn about the difficulties in negotiations and planning.

5 Curatorship III: Museum publications and cultural writings
The writings published for the museums share a common theme of identifying the artistic or historical values of the objects. In practice, however, there are various levels in addressing objects through literary practices. This lecture will look into some representative examples written for different targeted groups, which include children, pupils, and foreign visitors.

6 Museum and society I: Architecture and Design
Renovation projects were undertaken in numerous renowned museums around the world, because it has become aware that museums have to catch up with young generations and with the rapidly developed cities, in which they are located. This lecture will look into the case of Doha Quarter, where the locals took a deliberate attempt to a museum compound. From such we will consider the significance of architecture and museum collection.

7 Museum and society II: Education and Application of Digital Technology
Smartphones and other technological devices have become useful sources for museum presentations and visits. This lecture reviews some applications both within the museum building and outside it. The lecture will also respond to the concerns that digital technologies may exhaust public interests in the museum collections. With these both sides of argument, this lecture leads students to consider both the usefulness and shortcomings of digital technologies.

8 Museum and society III: Patrons, Donors and Volunteers, the sources of Funding and Support
Funding is always limited for cultural institutions. In this lecture, we will discuss the various potential fund sources for the museums. There is no standard way in the industry as to what the museum workers should do or write to apply for funding. But there are recognized sources about which they could get in touch: public visitors, membership programmes, volunteers, donars, and governmental resources. The key of the lecture is to develop an open mind to expand the coverage of funding from monetary values to public recognition.

9 Current issue I: Museum Branding and Marketing
Museum branding has been identified as a major marketing strategy for cultural institution. In this lecture, we will look at the suggestions of some successful museum workers and study their marketing approaches by creating a brand for museums.

10 Current issue II: Soft Power and the Political Roles of Museums.
As soon as Joseph Nye published his idea about soft power, which is based on the dissemination of cultural products or ideas, the discussion is always concerned with museum exhibitions. Museums could be taken as political statement of national treasures. In this lecture, we will discuss the ideas of Nye and look at how such concept has been interpreted in Asia.

11 Fieldtrip/site visit week
This week we will take some fresh air by visiting a local museum/site.

12 Current issue III: Museums and Contemporary Arts.
Should we include contemporary art in the context of museum? This is a difficult question that happens to most museums around the world. The well-used resolution is construction of a new venue dedicated to contemporary arts. The differences between the two disciplines—museums and contemporary arts—are related to the level of curatorship and public recognition. But the two themes seem to have blurred since the last decade.

13 Current issue IV: M+ and other museums in Hong Kong
In this last session, we will return our discussion to Hong Kong and look at what options this city has in comparison from what we have seen in other parts of the world. The M+ is dedicated to contemporary arts, or experimental arts; but the local museums remain fairly traditional in terms of collection and their range of exhibitions. Can Hong Kong develop a cultural hub? If so, what roles do these institutes would play?

Students will be led to examine aspects of the selected topic, conduct a critical literature review and a systematic investigation of the topic they have chosen. Students are required to regularly discuss with the teacher concrete findings in their research and submit written progress reports as scheduled by the teacher and a complete project report at the end of the term.

courses.description:
An opportunity for guided research and study of selected topics, such as: methodological approaches to the study of religion, theories of religion, folk religion, festivals, religion in transition in contemporary society, the Mother goddess, religion as salvation, ritual and sacrifice. The Special Topics will also be covering in Religion, Society and Contemporary Issues, including:
- Religion and Culture in Mesopotamia
- Religion and Economic Life
- Religion and Film
- Religion and Gender Studies
- Religion and Pluralistic Culture
- Religion and Politics
- Religions, Violence and Peace
- Religious Education and Developmental Psychology
- Religious Fundamentalism and Asian Cultures
- Any other topics related to Religion and Culture

Students are allowed to take this course not more than twice, and gain the units each time they pass the course.
courses.learning_outcomes:
To be able to engage in a critical analysis of religions by the methods of the humanities and social sciences including textual and historical analysis, philosophical and hermeneutical studies, and social and cultural analysis.

Ability to gather, organize, deploy, synthesize and evaluate different types of evidence and information.

Self-critical awareness of presuppositions and recognition that statements should be tested, that evidence and arguments are subject to assessment, that the interpreter’s role demands critical evaluation.

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Integrated Studies in Cultural Management
courses.description: This course is continuation of CUMT1001, Introduction to Cultural Management, and looks at cultural management from the macro perspective. Successful culture managers work to maintain healthy balance between culture and its relevant fields of interests, which sometimes could turn harmful or beneficial to the growth and development of the corresponding cultural area. The first session discusses the mutual dependence between culture and various areas, which include laws, economy, community, national interests, and art practitioners. In practice, cultural managers usually have to conform to the values and ethnics of the corresponding countries. For that reason, the second session will look at the approach taken in a few remarkable examples, which Taiwan, China, Singapore, South Korea, U.K., and the U.S.
courses.learning_outcomes: Upon completion of the course, students will be able to explain different national and social concerns for preserving culture; to identify the legal and ethnic issues relevant to cultural productions; to identify marketing strategies in cultural promotion; to evaluate the measures for maximizing the input of art practitioners; and to describe the various successful cultural schemes implemented in overseas countries.
courses.course_syllabus:
Section I: Culture in Context

1. Culture & States
The growth of cultural industries is strongly related to political environment and state policy. This lecture discusses the tension and mutual expectations between cultural workers and politicians. It will use the experience of the European Union and the US as examples.

2. Culture & Laws
Over the last few decades, there were a number of remarkable disputes over international art transactions and copy rights. Given the growing size of the art market, knowledge about the previous cases is essential. This lecture takes the examples taken from the US for discussions.

3. Culture & UNESCO
UNESCO is arguably a rising new power in forming a standard for evaluating cultural heritage of different nations. This lecture discusses the roles of UNESCO and its work in different cultural disciplines. National disputes over ownership of World Heritage sites will be bought up.

4. Culture & Community
Nowadays many nations and countries perceive culture and heritage as means to empower developing communities. This lecture discusses the conservation of indigenous or minorities cultures, and its impacts on the majority population of the same community.

5. Culture & Marketing
Culture and business are often taken as two contrasting disciplines. One of the key issues in cultural management is to develop cultural audience with both education and economic concerns. Religious sectors are a growing body of cultural venues for tourism and heritage management. This lecture discusses the debated concerns about conducting marketing or business-driven strategies in cultural and religious areas.

Section II: Case Studies
6. Cultural Management in Taiwan
Taiwan provides an excellent example to demonstrate the top-down organization of heritage sites. This lecture reviews a series of campaigns launched in Taiwan during the past twenty years, and discusses the reasons that may have contributed to engage the mass of Taiwan into different aspects of cultural industries.

7. Cultural Management in China
China represents many upcoming opportunities in cultural management. This lecture reviews some recent moves: rapid establishment of museums, growing number of commercial galleries and auction houses, rise of new Confucian schools, introduction of university degrees in cultural management, etc. The lecture discusses the consuming force underneath the cultural growth in the Mainland.

8. Cultural Management in Singapore
During the last two decades, the government of Singapore invested heavily on constructing cultural venues for both national and economic purposes. This lecture gives an overview of their establishment and invites students to reflect on the opportunities found in other areas of Asia.

9. Cultural Management in South Korea
This lecture discusses the contemporary cultural policies undertaken in South Korea. During the last decade, South Korea featured an economic miracle in Asia by rejuvenating the distressed financial market with cultural developments, which included the film industry, heritage protection, publication, and cultural tourism.

10. Cultural Management in UK
The United Kingdom was one of the earliest countries that invested heavily on promotion of cultural and arts events. This lecture gives a short history of the early developments made in the UK after the Second World War, and discusses the recent campaigns undertaken to break the boundaries between high arts and popular culture.

11. Cultural Management in USA
The United States distinguishes herself from all other countries by boosting the absence of a ministry for arts and culture, yet artists and cultural workers receive due respects for their contributions. This lecture looks at the case of the US and examines her success in cultural management.

12. Group presentation
This lecture is taken as a mock symposium for students to present their group projects and make comments on those by their peers. The presentation will be evaluated against the clarity and thoughts over the approved topics.

13. Conclusion: What is the best for Hong Kong?
This last session will summarize the topics covered throughout the course. Hong Kong is facing the challenge that many aspects of cultural developments in relation to urban planning, legislation, economy, national identity, and education are all pending. This lecture is an open discussion for students to brainstorm the possible approaches that Hong Kong could take in years to come.

courses.required_readings:
Week 1
Rosenstein, Carole. ‘ Cultural policy and the political nature of culture’ in J.P. Singh ed., International Cultural Policies and Power (Basingstoke, Palgrave Macmillan, 2010), pp. 19—28.
Dewey, Patricia. ‘Power in European Union Cultural Policy’ culture’ in J.P. Singh ed., International Cultural Policies and Power (Basingstoke, Palgrave Macmillan, 2010), pp. 113—126.

Week 2
Hoffman, Barbara. ‘International art transaction and resolution of art and cultural properties disputes: a United States perspective’ in B. Hoffman ed., Art and Cultural Heritage: Law Policy, and Practice (Cambridge ; New York : Cambridge University Press, 2006), pp. 159—177.

Week 3
Sun, Kerya Chau. ‘Angkor sites, cultural World Heritage’ in B. Hoffman ed., Art and Cultural Heritage: Law Policy, and Practice (Cambridge ; New York : Cambridge University Press, 2006), pp. 148—156.
Reap, James. ‘United States and the World Heritage Convention’ in B. Hoffman ed., Art and Cultural Heritage: Law Policy, and Practice (Cambridge ; New York : Cambridge University Press, 2006), pp. 234—238.

Week 4
Solomen, Maui. ‘Protecting Maori Heritage in New Zealand’ in B. Hoffman ed., Art and Cultural Heritage: Law Policy, and Practice (Cambridge ; New York : Cambridge University Press, 2006), pp. 352—362.

courses.subject: CURE
courses.title: Special Topic in Creative Industries
courses.description:
In recent years, the rapid development of cultural and creative industries has transformed the way cultural products are created, produced and distributed. These industries, from theater and fashion to social media and game design, highlight how contemporary societies use intellectual and creative approaches to gain economic and symbolic competitiveness. Alongside technological innovations, the creative sector also plays a significant role in driving global economic transformation, offering government agencies and entrepreneurs new strategies to produce creative experiences not anticipated in the past.

This course aims to provide students with both analytical and practical skills in cultural and creative industries. Students will engage with a diverse range of topics, exploring how various sectors within these industries inform a variety of theories and practices. By covering multiple creative and entrepreneurial strategies in the ever-expanding field of cultural and creative industries, this course will equip students with the ability to reflect on the current phenomenon and create projects that have the potential to impact individuals and societies.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Explain key historical and contemporary examples of creative industries, relating them to a broader local and global context
* Examine critical issues concerning how different cultural and economic models impact the creative industries
* Develop skills to critically analyze the relationship between culture, society and the economy
* Acquire personal and social competencies relating to the creative industries, and act as an active agent in producing creative projects
* Develop an attitude of innovation and creativity by challenging assumptions within the creative industries
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Historical Context in a Chosen Specific Industry

• Exploration of foundational concepts in the creative industries

• Terminologies relevant to this specific industry

2. Analytical Framework — Theories of Cultural Production in a Chosen Specific Industry

• Theories and analytical frameworks in contemporary practices within this specific industry

3. Policy and Governance in a Chosen Specific Industry

• The role of government policies in this specific industry

• The impact of regulatory frameworks and governance on growth and sustainability

4. Economic Impact in a Chosen Specific Industry

• Analysis of economic contributions to global and local economies in this specific industry

• Financial models and economic strategies

5. Management, Marketing Skills, and Organizational Behavior in a Chosen Specific Industry

• Management and marketing skills tailored for this specific industry

• Organizational behavior and strategies for effective leadership

6. Local and Global Phenomena in a Chosen Specific Industry

• The interplay between local cultural expressions and global market trends

7. Technological Impact in a Chosen Specific Industry

• Technological advancements in this specific industry

• Emerging technologies reshaping production, distribution, and consumption in this specific industry

8. Intellectual Property and Copyright Issues in a Chosen Specific Industry

• Legal frameworks surrounding intellectual property and copyright in this specific industry

9. Cultural Implications — Cultural Diversity, Privacy Issues, Equality, Labor, Environmental Issues, and Exploitation in a Chosen Specific Industry

• Cultural implications within this specific industry

10. Urban Revitalization in a Chosen Specific Industry

• The role of this specific industry in urban revitalization

• Urban economic and social renewal

11. Workshops x 3 — Forming Strategies and Project Planning

• Practical workshops to develop strategic and project planning skills

• Addressing contemporary challenges and opportunities in the chosen sector
